identity.title: Glass Product Manufacturing Made of Purchased Glass

lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market firms that repeatedly coat, laminate, temper, cut, bend, or otherwise shape purchased glass for external architectural, residential, transportation, appliance, furniture, or industrial customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal plants, non-control investment vehicles, primary glass melting, pure distribution, field installation without manufacturing, and consumer retail operations without a recurring external-customer fabrication business are excluded.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat business-to-business fabrication orders, often job-, unit-, square-foot-, or program-priced to drawings and performance specifications, with revenue from processed glass and associated fabrication rather than a separately metered labor service.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations for four-digit 327200 rather than wage-weighted task exposure in 327215. I map quoting, takeoff, drawing review, scheduling, nesting, purchasing, customer service, documentation, inspection triage, and machine monitoring to exposure while discounting loading, cutting, tempering, laminating, maintenance, packing, and delivery; primary glass segments in the broad source likely make its physical-production share higher than purchased-glass fabrication, biasing exposure downward.
a.rationale: Broad glass manufacturing was 55.08% production occupations in 2016, while office support was 8.50%, management 3.97%, engineering 2.79%, business and financial operations 2.22%, and sales 2.35%. Purchased-glass processors add information-heavy estimating, drawings, order entry, scheduling, and optimization, but safe handling and physical transformation still account for much of labor.
a.change_evidence: Current wage and task-time data from a representative set of independent 327215 fabricators, mapped to O*NET and separated among quoting, CAD, customer service, planning, quality, production, maintenance, logistics, and administration, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The occupational evidence is 2016 and four-digit rather than current six-digit data.
The DOE machine-learning example concerns primary flat-glass furnace control, not purchased-glass fabrication.

rho.rationale: Quoting, order capture, drawing extraction, nesting, production scheduling, document generation, and inspection support can be layered onto common ERP, CAD, and machine-vision workflows within five years. Fragmented data, custom orders, remake risk, safety glazing certification, legacy machines, integration costs, and required human review prevent full implementation.
rho.change_evidence: A survey of LMM fabricators covering ERP/CAD integration, digital drawings, machine connectivity, vision inspection, AI pilots, exception rates, implementation costs, and verified labor-hour savings by workflow would materially move the range.
caveats: No representative 327215 implementation-rate study was found.
Small custom shops and scaled automated fabricators will implement at very different rates.

e.rationale: The official scope is purchased-glass processing, a model commonly performed by independent regional fabricators with repeat external customers. Eligibility is reduced for captive divisions, installation-led firms, consumer retail operations, businesses with weak recurrence, and receipts-bridged candidates whose actual normalized EBITDA lies outside the target band.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census using D&B Hoovers, Thomasnet, company catalogs, permits, ownership records, customer mix, repeat-revenue data, and audited normalized EBITDA would materially move eligibility.
caveats: The injected n of 186 is an ESTIMATE derived from receipts and a sector margin rather than observed 327215 EBITDA.
Industry counts may mix standalone firms with establishments inside larger window, automotive, or building-products groups.

s5.rationale: No six-digit transfer cohort was found. A fragmented base of independent regional fabricators, owner succession, and strategic consolidation make control transfers plausible, while cyclicality, customer concentration, certification, equipment condition, working capital, and dependence on owner relationships suppress the qualifying rate.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, CapIQ, Axial, GF Data, trade publications, corporate records, and permit ownership changes, matched to the eligible firm denominator and verified to exclude closures, internal reorganizations, and asset-only sales, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Neither qualifying transfers nor the eligible denominator are directly observed.
Automotive, architectural, residential, and specialty processors likely have different buyer pools and succession patterns.

q.rationale: Fast quoting, short lead times, reliable tempering and lamination, certification, low remake rates, and freight proximity create service differentiation and allow some benefit retention. Repeat contractor, OEM, window, and distributor customers compare bids and renegotiate, so transparent productivity improvements and excess capacity can flow into price.
q.change_evidence: Executed supply agreements, bid histories, renewal cadence, price-cost waterfalls, remake charges, rush premiums, customer concentration, and win/loss data before and after documented automation would materially change retention.
caveats: No representative contract or causal cost-to-price dataset was available.
Retention should be lower in standardized commodity cutting and higher in qualified, complex, rapid-turn, or specialty fabrication.

d5.bridge: Federal Reserve/FRED output covers all four-digit glass products, while DOE evidence describes the building-window opportunity rather than constant-quality demand for all 327215 products. I anchor near recent broad sector output and add modest upside from retrofit and high-performance glazing; construction, vehicle, appliance, furniture, trade, and product-mix differences make the net error direction uncertain.
d5.rationale: Broad glass-products industrial production was 94.2941 in May 2026 on a 2017=100 basis, suggesting a subdued cyclical base rather than structural acceleration. DOE reports that windows account for about 10% of building energy use and identifies highly insulating and dynamic windows as development priorities, supporting retrofit and specification upside, while construction cycles, vehicle mix, imports, and alternative materials create downside.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-dollar fabricated-glass shipments and imports by architectural, residential, transportation, appliance, furniture, and specialty end market, paired with construction starts, retrofit spending, vehicle production, and customer forecasts, would materially move the ratio.
caveats: Four-digit production is not six-digit demand and can diverge through trade and inventory.
DOE's window energy statistic establishes a use-case opportunity, not the adoption rate or five-year fabricated-glass volume.

o.rationale: Remaining demand requires an accountable processor to convert physical stock glass to exact dimensions, coatings, laminates, temper, edgework, holes, bends, certification, packing, and delivery. Digital tools can automate decisions and customer interfaces, but the glass cannot be self-served or supplied entirely by software within five years.
o.change_evidence: Commercial lights-out fabrication benchmarks, equipment-vendor road maps, staffing and exception data after robotics and AI deployment, and customer acceptance of autonomously released safety glass would materially move this estimate.
caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than a measured operator-required share.
Automation can sharply reduce touches per unit without eliminating the accountable operating firm, which this primitive distinguishes.

narrative.executive_view: Purchased-glass fabrication is a comparatively coherent, fragmented repeat B2B model with many information-heavy workflows around complex physical orders. AI can assist quoting, drawings, scheduling, nesting, inspection, customer service, and administration, while the glass transformation and accountable quality release remain physical and operator-required.
narrative.ai_impact: The most implementable opportunities sit before and around the line: extracting specifications, estimating, optimizing cuts, promising dates, sequencing loads, generating paperwork, triaging defects, and analyzing downtime. Custom geometry, safety-glass requirements, remake cost, legacy equipment, and material handling keep humans in approval and execution loops.
narrative.value_capture: Speed, reliability, low remakes, certification, specialty capabilities, and freight proximity create value beyond raw processing cost. Customers still compare bids and rebid repeat work, so some benefits are passed through, but rapid-turn and qualified fabricators should retain more than commodity job shops.
narrative.firm_availability: The injected LMM estimate of 186 firms suggests a meaningful possible pool, but it must be filtered for independent ownership, repeat external revenue, manufacturing rather than installation or retail, and actual normalized EBITDA. No measured 327215 control-transfer cohort was available.
narrative.demand_durability: Architectural, window, transportation, appliance, furniture, and specialty end markets preserve a broad physical demand base. High-performance and retrofit glazing provide upside, while construction and auto cycles, imports, substitution, and customer insourcing create a wide range around modest growth.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main gaps are six-digit task data, firm eligibility, realized implementation, contractual pass-through, and transfers. Construction exposure, customer concentration, breakage and remakes, product liability, certification, equipment bottlenecks, glass input and freight volatility, and working capital can outweigh software gains.
narrative.principal_driver: A sizable independent-fabricator pool combines repeat customer relationships with digitizable quoting, planning, and quality workflows around an irreducibly physical product.
narrative.principal_weakness: Cyclical end markets and unobserved firm-level eligibility, EBITDA, transfer rates, and pass-through leave the scale of attainable opportunity uncertain.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 327215 Glass Product Manufacturing Made of Purchased Glass
sources.supports: Defines 327215 as establishments primarily engaged in coating, laminating, tempering, or shaping purchased glass.

sources.title: May 2016 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 327200
sources.supports: Reports 85,250 jobs and occupation shares including production 55.08%, office support 8.50%, management 3.97%, engineering 2.79%, business and financial operations 2.22%, and sales 2.35%.

sources.title: High Performance Computing Improves Manufacturing Productivity and Competitiveness in the Glass Industry
sources.supports: Adjacent glass-process evidence that machine learning can convert computational modeling into real-time operating guidance; the documented surrogate moved analysis from up to two weeks to minutes.

sources.title: Department of Energy Windows Technologies Subprogram
sources.supports: Reports that windows account for about 10% of building energy use and influence end uses comprising 40%, and identifies highly insulating windows, dynamic solar control, and daylighting as development areas.

sources.title: Industrial Production: Glass and Glass Product Manufacturing (NAICS 3272)
sources.supports: Federal Reserve broad real-output proxy; May 2026 was 94.2941 on a 2017=100 index.
